_meta.authoringNotes: Classification per Seyberth HW Pediatr Nephrol 2008 (PMID 18504634). Clinical management per KDIGO working group and Seyberth 2011 review. Genetic basis from Konrad et al. 2009 and Kleta & Bockenhauer 2006. Epidemiology estimates from European cohort data (van der Wijst et al. 2019).

h1Title: Bartter Syndrome: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Bartter syndrome is a group of rare autosomal-recessive salt-wasting tubulopathies caused by mutations in ion transporters or channels in the thick ascending limb of the loop of Henle, producing a phenotype of persistent hypokalemia, hypochloremic metabolic alkalosis, elevated renin and aldosterone, and normal-to-low blood pressure. The estimated prevalence is 1-2 per million people. Antenatal Bartter syndrome (types I-III) presents in utero with polyhydramnios and causes life-threatening electrolyte crises in the newborn period; classic Bartter syndrome (type III-V) presents in infancy or childhood with polyuria, polydipsia, and growth failure. Oral potassium supplementation, prostaglandin synthesis inhibitors (indomethacin), and potassium-sparing diuretics form the core of lifelong management.

keyStats.prevalence: Estimated 1-2 per million people in European populations; exact global figures unavailable due to rarity and underdiagnosis
keyStats.demographics: Affects males and females equally; onset before age 5 in most cases; antenatal forms may be identified on second-trimester ultrasound by polyhydramnios
keyStats.avgAge: Antenatal/neonatal Bartter syndrome presents from birth; classic Bartter syndrome typically diagnosed in first 2 years of life; some mild cases diagnosed in adulthood
keyStats.globalCases: Fewer than 1,000 documented cases in published literature as of 2020; consanguineous populations in parts of the Middle East and North Africa have higher reported frequencies

definition: Bartter syndrome (ICD-10: E26.81) is a group of inherited renal tubular disorders caused by loss-of-function mutations in proteins that form or regulate the sodium-potassium-chloride co-transporter 2 (NKCC2) complex in the thick ascending limb (TAL) of Henle's loop. At least five genetically distinct types are recognized: type I (SLC12A1 mutation, NKCC2 deficiency), type II (KCNJ1 mutation, ROMK potassium channel deficiency), type III (CLCNKB mutation, chloride channel ClC-Kb deficiency), type IV (BSND mutation, barttin subunit deficiency associated with sensorineural deafness), and type V (CASR gain-of-function mutation causing activated calcium-sensing receptor). The common pathophysiology is failure of sodium chloride reabsorption in the TAL, leading to urinary salt wasting, volume depletion, secondary hyperaldosteronism, hypokalemia, and metabolic alkalosis. High prostaglandin E2 production is a key mediator of symptoms in antenatal forms. Bartter syndrome must be distinguished from Gitelman syndrome (distal tubular defect) and from surreptitious diuretic use. The condition is managed primarily by pediatric or adult nephrology and endocrinology.

typesClassification.type: Type I Bartter syndrome (NKCC2 defect, SLC12A1)
typesClassification.description: Loss-of-function mutation in the apical NKCC2 co-transporter completely abolishes sodium-potassium-chloride reabsorption in the TAL. Severe antenatal form with polyhydramnios, premature delivery, neonatal salt-wasting crisis, nephrocalcinosis, and hypercalciuria. Earliest onset and most severe natural history.

typesClassification.type: Type II Bartter syndrome (ROMK defect, KCNJ1)
typesClassification.description: ROMK potassium channel mutation; potassium recycling across the apical membrane is impaired, secondarily reducing NKCC2 function. Antenatal polyhydramnios and neonatal salt wasting; paradoxically, ROMK-null neonates may initially be hyperkalemic before developing chronic hypokalemia — a diagnostic pitfall.

typesClassification.type: Type III Bartter syndrome (ClC-Kb defect, CLCNKB)
typesClassification.description: Basolateral chloride channel mutation affecting both the TAL and the distal convoluted tubule. Broader phenotypic spectrum ranging from severe antenatal presentation to mild classic form resembling Gitelman syndrome in adults. The most common genotype in European series.

typesClassification.type: Type IV Bartter syndrome (barttin defect, BSND)
typesClassification.description: Loss of barttin, the beta-subunit required for both ClC-Ka and ClC-Kb chloride channels. Causes profound antenatal salt wasting combined with sensorineural deafness (barttin is also expressed in cochlear endolymphatic cells) and chronic renal failure. Most severe prognosis of all types.

Activating mutations in the calcium-sensing receptor cause hypocalcemia, hypomagnesemia, and Bartter-like tubulopathy. Unlike other types, inheritance may be autosomal-dominant. Associated with hypocalcemic hypoparathyroidism.

primarySymptoms: Profound polyuria (urine output above 3-5 L/day in adults; proportionally higher in infants) from urinary salt and water wasting, causing constant thirst and polydipsia.
Severe hypokalemia (serum potassium below 3.0 mmol/L) producing muscle weakness, cramps, fatigue, and in severe cases, paralytic ileus or rhabdomyolysis.
Hypochloremic metabolic alkalosis on blood gas analysis, reflecting both chloride wasting and secondary aldosterone-driven hydrogen ion excretion in the distal nephron.
Growth failure and short stature in children from chronic volume depletion, caloric deficiency secondary to anorexia and vomiting, and electrolyte disturbance impairing growth hormone signaling.
Recurrent vomiting and anorexia in infants, often leading to failure to thrive before the diagnosis of Bartter syndrome is established.
Polyhydramnios during pregnancy, visible on second-trimester ultrasound, from fetal polyuria — the first sign of antenatal Bartter syndrome (types I, II, IV).
Neonatal salt-wasting crisis with dehydration, hypotension, and hyperkalemia in type II neonates — an acute emergency in the first days of life.
Sensorineural deafness in type IV Bartter syndrome, present from birth, from barttin deficiency in cochlear endolymph cells.
Nephrocalcinosis — calcification of the renal medulla visible on renal ultrasound — from chronic hypercalciuria caused by high loop prostaglandin activity, particularly in types I and II.
Normal-to-low blood pressure despite markedly elevated renin and aldosterone, distinguishing Bartter syndrome from primary hyperaldosteronism where hypertension is expected.
earlyWarningSigns: Unexplained hypokalemia below 3.0 mmol/L with metabolic alkalosis in an infant or child not receiving diuretics — the triad that defines Bartter syndrome until proven otherwise.
Polyhydramnios on second-trimester fetal ultrasound with elevated amniotic fluid alpha-fetoprotein, prompting investigation for antenatal Bartter syndrome.
Failure to thrive, anorexia, and recurrent vomiting in an infant younger than 12 months with normal GI workup but low urinary specific gravity.
Unexplained hypokalemia with urinary potassium wasting (urinary potassium-to-creatinine ratio above 2.5) and normal blood pressure in an older child or adult.
Nephrocalcinosis on renal ultrasound in an infant with unexplained polyuria — prompting electrolyte, renin, and aldosterone testing.
emergencySigns: Severe hypokalemia below 2.5 mmol/L with ECG changes (flattened T waves, U waves, prolonged QT) — requires urgent IV potassium replacement under cardiac monitoring.
Neonatal salt-wasting crisis with severe dehydration, metabolic acidosis, and hemodynamic instability in the first days of life — IV saline and potassium chloride are required urgently.
Rhabdomyolysis from severe hypokalemia (CK above 10,000 U/L) with acute kidney injury — requires IV hydration and potassium replacement plus monitoring for myoglobinuria.
Respiratory muscle weakness leading to hypoventilation in severe, untreated hypokalemia — a rare but life-threatening complication requiring ICU support.

causes.cause: SLC12A1 mutation (type I): loss of NKCC2 transporter function
causes.description: Homozygous or compound heterozygous loss-of-function mutations in SLC12A1 completely abrogate the apical NKCC2 sodium-potassium-chloride co-transporter in the thick ascending limb. The result is complete failure of salt reabsorption at this nephron segment, causing massive urinary sodium, chloride, potassium, and calcium losses from birth.

causes.cause: KCNJ1 mutation (type II): loss of ROMK channel function
causes.description: ROMK potassium channels recycle potassium from cells back into the tubular lumen, maintaining a positive lumen potential that drives paracellular magnesium and calcium reabsorption. Loss of ROMK removes the electrochemical driving force for NKCC2 function. Initial paradoxical hyperkalemia in neonates reflects ROMK's dual role in cortical collecting duct potassium secretion.

causes.cause: CLCNKB mutation (type III): loss of basolateral ClC-Kb chloride channel
causes.description: Chloride absorbed by NKCC2 exits the basolateral membrane via ClC-Kb. Loss of this channel causes chloride accumulation in the cell, creating a feedback loop that reduces NKCC2 activity. ClC-Kb is also expressed in the distal convoluted tubule, explaining why type III presents similarly to Gitelman syndrome in some patients.

causes.cause: BSND mutation (type IV): loss of barttin
causes.description: Barttin is the beta-subunit required for trafficking and function of both ClC-Ka and ClC-Kb channels. Without barttin, both basolateral chloride channels in the TAL and inner medullary collecting duct fail, producing the most severe salt-wasting phenotype. Barttin is also required in cochlear endolymphatic cells, causing sensorineural deafness.

causes.cause: CASR gain-of-function (type V): activating calcium-sensing receptor mutation
causes.description: Activating mutations in the calcium-sensing receptor (CaSR) suppress PTH secretion and directly inhibit NKCC2 activity in the TAL via CaSR expressed on tubular cells. Resulting hypomagnesemia and hypocalcemia with Bartter-like features. Some cases are autosomal-dominant and can be diagnosed in adults.

causes.cause: High prostaglandin E2 as mediator of antenatal and neonatal disease
causes.description: Salt-sensing mechanisms in the macula densa are disrupted by TAL dysfunction; the macula densa responds with excess prostaglandin E2 production, which amplifies renal blood flow, renin release, and tubular dysfunction. This is the target of indomethacin therapy, which substantially reduces polyuria and electrolyte losses in types I-III.

riskFactors.description: Types I-IV are autosomal-recessive; both parents must carry a heterozygous mutation (carrier state). In consanguineous families (first-cousin marriage), carrier rates are elevated and risk of an affected child is substantially higher — up to 25% per pregnancy when both parents are carriers.

Bartter syndrome is overrepresented in communities with high rates of consanguineous marriage, including parts of the Middle East, North Africa, and South Asia. Population studies in Saudi Arabia and Kuwait report higher case frequencies than European series.

riskFactors.factor: Positive family history of Bartter syndrome
riskFactors.description: Siblings of an affected child have a 25% risk if both parents are carriers. Prenatal genetic testing is available for families with a known pathogenic variant.

Type V can be autosomal-dominant; a parent with a de novo or inherited CASR gain-of-function mutation has a 50% chance of transmitting to offspring. Maternal hypercalciuric hypocalcemia during pregnancy can exacerbate fetal CASR-mediated hypomagnesemia.

Polyhydramnios of uncertain cause on antenatal ultrasound increases the pre-test probability of antenatal Bartter syndrome, particularly when associated with echogenic bowel or bilateral hydronephrosis.
Any child with unexplained hypokalemia and metabolic alkalosis, particularly with high urinary potassium wasting and normal blood pressure, requires evaluation for Bartter or Gitelman syndrome.

affectedDemographics: Males and females are equally affected by Bartter syndrome, consistent with the autosomal-recessive inheritance pattern of types I-IV.

Consanguineous populations in the Middle East (particularly Saudi Arabia, Kuwait, and Iran) have higher reported rates of Bartter syndrome than European populations, reflecting founder mutations and consanguinity.
Antenatal Bartter syndrome is detected prenatally in the second trimester in centers where fetal polyhydramnios triggers electrolyte and genetic evaluation.

A minority of patients — primarily those with type III (ClC-Kb) or type V (CaSR) mutations — are diagnosed in adulthood, often after years of unexplained hypokalemia or nephrolithiasis attributed to other causes.
diagnosisOverview: The diagnostic approach starts with the biochemical triad: hypokalemia, hypochloremic metabolic alkalosis, and elevated plasma renin and aldosterone in the context of normal or low blood pressure. Urinary potassium-to-creatinine ratio above 2.5 mmol/mmol confirms renal potassium wasting rather than gastrointestinal losses. Urinary chloride above 20 mmol/L rules out vomiting as the cause. Once the biochemical pattern is established, secondary causes must be excluded — particularly surreptitious diuretic use (urine diuretic screen), which produces an identical biochemical picture. Primary hyperaldosteronism is excluded by the elevated renin. Renal ultrasound is performed to assess nephrocalcinosis (types I-II) and renal size. 24-hour urine calcium is elevated in types I and II (hypercalciuria). Prostaglandin E2 (urine PGE2) is elevated in antenatal and classic Bartter syndrome and normalizes with indomethacin — a useful therapeutic marker. Genetic confirmation by next-generation sequencing panel targeting SLC12A1, KCNJ1, CLCNKB, BSND, and CASR is strongly recommended for all cases and is essential for genetic counseling, prenatal diagnosis, and for distinguishing type III from Gitelman syndrome. Audiological evaluation (auditory brainstem response) is mandatory when type IV is suspected.

diagnosticTests.test: Serum electrolytes, blood gas, and creatinine
diagnosticTests.purpose: Establishes hypokalemia, hypochloremic metabolic alkalosis, and baseline kidney function — the biochemical hallmark of Bartter syndrome
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draw. Results in 1-2 hours. Potassium typically below 3.0 mmol/L; bicarbonate above 30 mmol/L; chloride below 100 mmol/L; blood pH above 7.45.

diagnosticTests.test: Plasma renin activity and serum aldosterone
diagnosticTests.purpose: Both are markedly elevated in Bartter syndrome, confirming secondary hyperaldosteronism from volume depletion
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood draw in the morning after 2 hours of upright posture for standard conditions. Renin and aldosterone are very high, distinguishing Bartter syndrome from primary hyperaldosteronism (where renin is suppressed).

diagnosticTests.test: Urine electrolytes (spot or 24-hour), urine potassium-to-creatinine ratio
diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms renal potassium wasting rather than GI losses; elevated urine chloride excludes vomiting as the cause
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Spot urine sample; can be measured in any urine sample. Urinary potassium-to-creatinine ratio above 2.5 mmol/mmol confirms renal potassium wasting. Results available within 1-2 hours.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Excludes surreptitious diuretic use (thiazides, loop diuretics), which produces an identical biochemical picture
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Spot urine sample sent for immunoassay or LC-MS/MS for common diuretics. Results in 1-3 days from reference lab. A positive result is a common mimicker of Bartter syndrome.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies nephrocalcinosis (particularly in types I and II), renal length, and structural abnormalities
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Painless 20-minute ultrasound. Medullary nephrocalcinosis appears as increased echogenicity in the pyramids. Detected in 30-50% of type I/II patients. Can be performed even in neonates.

diagnosticTests.test: Genetic sequencing panel (SLC12A1, KCNJ1, CLCNKB, BSND, CASR)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms the specific genetic subtype, guides prognosis, enables prenatal counseling, and distinguishes type III from Gitelman syndrome
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood or saliva sample sent to a specialist genetics laboratory. Results in 3-6 weeks. Identifies the pathogenic variant in about 80-85% of phenotypically classic cases.

treatmentOverview: Treatment of Bartter syndrome is lifelong symptomatic management — there is no curative therapy for the underlying channelopathy. The goals are to maintain serum potassium above 3.0-3.5 mmol/L, correct alkalosis, minimize polyuria, and support normal growth. Oral potassium chloride supplementation is the foundation, typically 1-5 mmol/kg/day in infants and children, titrated to serum potassium. Indomethacin — a non-selective COX inhibitor — reduces prostaglandin E2 synthesis and substantially decreases polyuria, polydipsia, urinary electrolyte losses, and renal renin-angiotensin activation in antenatal and classic Bartter syndrome (types I-III). Starting dose is typically 0.5-1.0 mg/kg/day in two to three divided doses with food; GI and renal side effects require monitoring. Indomethacin is the most effective single agent and can normalize growth velocity in children when introduced early. Rofecoxib and celecoxib (COX-2-selective) are used in patients with GI intolerance to indomethacin, with similar efficacy. Potassium-sparing diuretics — spironolactone (2-4 mg/kg/day) and amiloride (0.3 mg/kg/day) — block aldosterone-driven renal potassium losses in the distal nephron and are useful adjuncts, especially in older patients. RAAS blockade with ACE inhibitors or angiotensin receptor blockers reduces aldosterone-driven potassium loss but can cause hypotension. Magnesium supplementation is required in type III/V patients with hypomagnesemia. Neonates with life-threatening salt-wasting require IV normal saline with potassium chloride for stabilization before oral therapy is feasible. Renal transplantation in type IV patients with progressive chronic kidney disease corrects the biochemical phenotype because transplanted kidneys carry normal tubular function, though cochlear deafness is not reversed.

medicalTreatments.name: Oral potassium chloride supplementation (1-5 mmol/kg/day in children)
medicalTreatments.description: Foundation of electrolyte management; titrated to maintain serum potassium above 3.0-3.5 mmol/L. Given in multiple divided doses with food. Slow-release preparations improve tolerance. Dose requirements are high and often surprising — large losses continue throughout life.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Maintains target potassium in most patients when combined with other agents; difficult to achieve without adjunctive indomethacin in types I-II

medicalTreatments.name: Indomethacin (0.5-2 mg/kg/day in 2-3 divided doses)
medicalTreatments.description: Non-selective COX inhibitor that reduces prostaglandin E2 production in the macula densa and throughout the thick ascending limb. Substantially decreases polyuria, normalizes growth, and reduces electrolyte losses in types I-III. Given with food to reduce GI side effects. Renal function and GI symptoms monitored regularly.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces urine output by 30-60%, normalizes growth velocity in most treated children; long-term GI and renal monitoring required

medicalTreatments.name: Amiloride (0.3-0.5 mg/kg/day oral) or spironolactone (2-4 mg/kg/day)
medicalTreatments.description: Potassium-sparing diuretics that reduce aldosterone-driven distal potassium secretion. Amiloride acts directly on the epithelial sodium channel (ENaC); spironolactone blocks the aldosterone receptor. Both are useful adjuncts to reduce oral potassium replacement requirements.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces urinary potassium losses by 20-40% and lowers daily potassium supplement requirements

medicalTreatments.name: IV potassium chloride (acute resuscitation, closely monitored)
medicalTreatments.description: Required for neonatal salt-wasting crises and when serum potassium is below 2.5 mmol/L with ECG changes. Administered as dilute IV solution (not exceeding 10-20 mmol/h peripherally) under cardiac monitoring.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Corrects acute hypokalemia within hours; must be combined with volume replacement and oral maintenance to prevent recurrence

medicalTreatments.name: Magnesium supplementation (oral magnesium glycerophosphate or chloride)
medicalTreatments.description: Required in types III and V where hypomagnesemia is present. Magnesium deficiency worsens hypokalemia by impairing cellular potassium retention. Target serum magnesium above 0.7 mmol/L.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Corrects co-existing hypomagnesemia, which improves potassium retention and reduces cramping

medicalTreatments.name: Oral rehydration solution (in infants) or increased dietary sodium
medicalTreatments.description: Liberal sodium intake partially counteracts urinary salt wasting in infants and children. No sodium restriction should be imposed. Oral rehydration solutions provide sodium, potassium, and water in proportions suited to the electrolyte profile.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Supports volume status and growth in infants; reduces the frequency of dehydration episodes

surgicalOptions.name: Renal transplantation (type IV with chronic renal failure)
surgicalOptions.description: Type IV Bartter syndrome progressively damages the kidneys and eventually requires renal replacement therapy. Transplantation corrects the biochemical phenotype because the donated kidney has normal tubular transporters. Sensorineural deafness is not reversed by transplantation.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Graft survival in Bartter syndrome type IV is comparable to other non-immunological causes of ESRD; long-term potassium and electrolyte homeostasis restored in graft recipients in published series

alternativeTreatments.name: COX-2-selective inhibitors (rofecoxib, celecoxib)
alternativeTreatments.description: Used when non-selective NSAIDs such as indomethacin cause gastrointestinal side effects. COX-2-selective agents have similar efficacy for reducing prostaglandin E2 in Bartter syndrome with a lower GI risk profile, though renal monitoring is still required.

whySeeSpecialist: All patients with Bartter syndrome require long-term management by a pediatric or adult nephrologist experienced in rare tubular disorders. Endocrinology input is useful for managing secondary hormonal abnormalities. Genetics consultation is essential for molecular confirmation, family counseling, and prenatal diagnosis planning. Type IV patients require audiology referral.
doctorSelectionGuide: Seek a center with expertise in inherited tubulopathies — major pediatric nephrology units and ERKNet (European Reference Network for Rare Kidney Diseases) centers in Europe, or equivalent nephrology centers in North America. Genetic confirmation requires a laboratory offering next-generation sequencing for tubulopathies.
hospitalCriteria: Pediatric or adult nephrology with experience managing tubulopathies
Molecular genetics laboratory for SLC12A1, KCNJ1, CLCNKB, BSND, CASR sequencing
Renal ultrasound capability for nephrocalcinosis monitoring
Audiological evaluation service for type IV Bartter syndrome
Access to indomethacin, potassium supplements, and IV electrolyte replacement
keyFacilities: Rare kidney disease specialist centers (ERKNet-certified in Europe)
Pediatric nephrology units with inherited tubular disorder programs
Clinical genetics departments for molecular diagnosis and family counseling
Audiological services for type IV patients
Renal transplant units for type IV with progressive CKD
preventionStrategies: Genetic counseling for couples with a previously affected child or a known carrier parent — quantifies the 25% recurrence risk per pregnancy and guides prenatal options.
Prenatal genetic testing (chorionic villus sampling or amniocentesis) when both parents carry a confirmed pathogenic variant in SLC12A1, KCNJ1, CLCNKB, or BSND.
Early neonatal electrolyte screening in newborns with polyhydramnios or at risk by family history to detect neonatal salt-wasting crisis before hemodynamic compromise.
Liberal sodium and potassium intake from the outset — no dietary sodium or potassium restriction — to minimize salt-wasting symptoms.
Avoid medications that further inhibit renal sodium or potassium conservation (thiazides, loop diuretics) unless there is a separate indication, as they can precipitate acute worsening.
lifestyleModifications: Take electrolyte supplements as prescribed on schedule — dose omissions quickly lead to recurrent hypokalemia with fatigue and cramping.
Maintain high fluid intake, especially in hot weather, fever, or diarrhea, to prevent dehydration when urinary water losses are obligatory and cannot be reduced.
Carry an emergency card or wear medical alert identification documenting Bartter syndrome, baseline electrolyte values, and the need for potassium and saline replacement in emergencies.
Monitor serum potassium, sodium, bicarbonate, and renal function every 3-6 months; more frequently in acute illness or after dose changes.
Avoid high environmental temperatures and prolonged physical exertion without extra fluid and electrolyte intake.
Engage school nurses or workplaces in awareness of the need for flexible bathroom access (due to polyuria) and electrolyte supplement schedules.

recommended: High-potassium foods (bananas, dried fruit, potatoes, avocado, dairy) to partially offset urinary losses alongside oral potassium supplements
Adequate dietary sodium — no restriction; mildly liberal sodium intake supports intravascular volume
Magnesium-rich foods (nuts, seeds, dark chocolate, leafy greens) particularly in type III and V patients
Generous fluid intake throughout the day — at least 1.5-2× standard age-appropriate recommendations
avoid: NSAIDs other than prescribed indomethacin without physician approval — unprescribed self-medication may affect renal function
Caffeinated beverages in excess, which have a mild diuretic effect and worsen polyuria
Sodium-restricted diets, which exacerbate volume depletion and renin-aldosterone activation
Vigorous physical activity without extra potassium and fluid supplementation

exerciseGuidelines: Moderate physical activity is encouraged and important for bone health and cardiovascular fitness in children and adults with Bartter syndrome. Intense or prolonged exercise in heat requires pre-emptive extra fluid and potassium intake, as sweat losses combine with obligatory urinary losses to quickly deplete potassium and trigger weakness. Contact sports are not contraindicated but risk of rhabdomyolysis during severe hypokalemia warrants caution.
dailyManagement: Take oral potassium chloride and indomethacin at scheduled times, with food, to minimize GI side effects.
Check home blood pressure — consistently low values may indicate volume depletion requiring sodium or fluid increase.
Monitor daily fluid intake and urine output (or wet nappy counts in infants) as a proxy for volume status.
Keep an emergency supply of oral rehydration solution and potassium supplements at home, work, and when traveling.
Attend 3-6 monthly laboratory monitoring for electrolytes, creatinine, and blood gas.
Report new muscle weakness, irregular heartbeat, or extreme thirst and urination increase to the care team — these signal deteriorating electrolyte balance.
prognosis: Prognosis in Bartter syndrome depends heavily on genotype and the timing and quality of treatment. Types I-III (with prompt treatment including indomethacin and potassium supplementation) allow near-normal growth and development in the majority of children. Long-term renal function is generally preserved in types I-III with adequate management, though nephrocalcinosis may cause mild reduction in GFR over decades. Type IV carries the poorest prognosis: a substantial proportion of patients develop chronic kidney disease (CKD) stage 3-4 by adulthood and require dialysis or renal transplantation in the third to fifth decade. Sensorineural deafness in type IV is permanent and not corrected by renal transplantation. Type V outcomes depend on the severity of the activating CaSR mutation; mild cases may be managed with conservative electrolyte replacement. Delayed diagnosis in infancy is associated with irreversible growth failure. With early diagnosis and optimal therapy, most patients can attend mainstream schooling, pursue employment, and have a reasonable quality of life.
recoveryTimeline: In neonatal crisis, electrolyte correction takes 24-72 hours with IV therapy, followed by transition to oral replacement. Growth velocity improves within 3-6 months of starting indomethacin. Serum potassium stabilizes within 2-4 weeks of optimized oral supplementation. Nephrocalcinosis does not regress but may stabilize on treatment. Long-term management is lifelong.
complications: Nephrocalcinosis and progressive chronic kidney disease, particularly in types I and II with persistent hypercalciuria — monitor with annual GFR and renal ultrasound.
Growth failure and short stature when diagnosis is delayed beyond the first year of life or when electrolyte control is poor during critical growth years.
Sensorineural deafness in type IV — permanent and detected by auditory brainstem response testing in neonates.
Rhabdomyolysis from severe acute hypokalemia — causes acute kidney injury and myoglobinuria; requires urgent potassium replacement and IV fluid resuscitation.
GI complications of long-term indomethacin including peptic ulcer disease, GI bleeding, and intestinal perforation — mitigated by proton pump inhibitor co-prescription.
Renal transplant requirement in type IV — all aspects of end-stage renal disease preparation and immunosuppression management apply.

supportResources.name: ERKNet — European Reference Network for Rare Kidney Diseases
supportResources.description: EU reference network linking specialist centers for rare kidney diseases including Bartter syndrome; provides expert consultation, guideline development, and patient registry.

supportResources.name: NORD — National Organization for Rare Disorders (Bartter Syndrome)
supportResources.description: US rare disease organization providing patient guides, physician referral resources, and clinical trial information for Bartter syndrome.

supportResources.name: Kidney Disease: Improving Global Outcomes (KDIGO)
supportResources.description: International nephrology guideline body whose clinical practice guidelines for electrolyte disorders and tubular disease inform Bartter syndrome management worldwide.

confusedWithConditions.name: Chronic kidney disease
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: CKD causes hypokalemia only when associated with renal tubular acidosis and is typically accompanied by elevated creatinine, reduced GFR, and hypertension. Bartter syndrome presents with normal or low blood pressure, a primary tubular defect with normal early GFR, and markedly elevated renin and aldosterone that are not expected in CKD-related hypokalemia.

confusedWithConditions.name: Metabolic alkalosis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Metabolic alkalosis from vomiting or antacid use is clinically similar to Bartter syndrome biochemically, but urinary chloride below 20 mmol/L and low urine potassium distinguish vomiting-induced alkalosis, whereas Bartter syndrome shows high urine chloride and potassium and elevated renin.

confusedWithConditions.name: Renal tubular acidosis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Distal (type 1) renal tubular acidosis also causes hypokalemia and renal tubular dysfunction, but the acid-base disturbance is acidosis rather than alkalosis, urine pH is inappropriately high, and the mutation affects collecting duct H+-ATPase rather than TAL transporters.

relatedConditions.name: Metabolic alkalosis
relatedConditions.relevance: Hypochloremic metabolic alkalosis is the defining acid-base consequence of Bartter syndrome and the framework through which the differential diagnosis is organized.

relatedConditions.name: Metabolic acidosis
relatedConditions.relevance: Understanding the acid-base opposite (acidosis from distal RTA) clarifies why Bartter syndrome causes alkalosis via aldosterone-driven proton secretion in the collecting duct.

relatedConditions.name: Alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency
relatedConditions.relevance: Both are rare single-gene recessive disorders requiring genetic counseling, molecular diagnosis, and specialist center management — illustrating shared management principles for rare metabolic conditions.

relatedConditions.name: Pituitary disorders
relatedConditions.relevance: Secondary hyperaldosteronism in Bartter syndrome superficially resembles primary aldosteronism from adrenal/pituitary pathology; differentiating renin-driven from ACTH/pituitary-driven endocrine disorders is part of the diagnostic pathway.

faqs.question: What is Bartter syndrome?
faqs.answer: Bartter syndrome is a rare inherited kidney disorder caused by mutations in salt-transporting proteins in the loop of Henle. It causes persistent low potassium (hypokalemia), metabolic alkalosis, and normal or low blood pressure despite very high renin and aldosterone levels.

faqs.question: How is Bartter syndrome inherited?
faqs.answer: Types I-IV are autosomal-recessive — both copies of the gene must be mutated for the disease to occur. Parents are usually unaffected carriers. Each pregnancy carries a 25% risk of an affected child when both parents carry the same mutation. Type V can be autosomal-dominant.

faqs.question: What are the symptoms of Bartter syndrome?
faqs.answer: Main symptoms are excessive thirst and urination (polyuria/polydipsia), muscle weakness and cramps from low potassium, growth failure in children, recurrent vomiting, and fatigue. Antenatal forms cause excess amniotic fluid (polyhydramnios) during pregnancy. Blood pressure is normal or low despite high renin.

faqs.question: How is Bartter syndrome diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis requires blood tests showing low potassium, metabolic alkalosis, and high renin and aldosterone with normal blood pressure, plus elevated urinary potassium despite hypokalemia. A urine diuretic screen excludes diuretic use. Genetic testing identifies the specific mutation.

faqs.question: What is the treatment for Bartter syndrome?
faqs.answer: Treatment includes oral potassium chloride supplements (high doses), indomethacin (prostaglandin inhibitor that reduces polyuria), and potassium-sparing diuretics (amiloride or spironolactone). There is no cure; treatment is lifelong and managed by a nephrologist.

faqs.question: How many types of Bartter syndrome are there?
faqs.answer: Five genetic types are defined: type I (NKCC2 mutation), type II (ROMK mutation), type III (ClC-Kb mutation), type IV (barttin mutation, with sensorineural deafness), and type V (activating CaSR mutation). Types I-II are the most severe; type III has the broadest clinical spectrum.

faqs.question: What is the difference between Bartter syndrome and Gitelman syndrome?
faqs.answer: Both are inherited tubular disorders causing hypokalemic alkalosis, but Gitelman syndrome is caused by a distal tubule defect (SLC12A3 mutation), presents in older children or adults, causes hypomagnesemia and hypocalciuria, and is milder. Bartter syndrome affects the loop of Henle and presents in infancy with hypercalciuria.

faqs.question: Can Bartter syndrome be detected before birth?
faqs.answer: Antenatal Bartter syndrome (types I-II) can be suspected from polyhydramnios on second-trimester ultrasound caused by fetal polyuria. Genetic testing of chorionic villus sampling or amniotic fluid can confirm the diagnosis in families with known mutations.

faqs.question: What causes low potassium in Bartter syndrome?
faqs.answer: Defective salt transport in the loop of Henle causes urinary sodium and chloride wasting, activating renin and aldosterone. High aldosterone drives potassium secretion in the collecting duct, causing massive urinary potassium losses that exceed dietary intake, producing persistent hypokalemia.

faqs.question: Does Bartter syndrome affect blood pressure?
faqs.answer: Unlike most causes of high renin and aldosterone, Bartter syndrome does not cause hypertension. Salt wasting keeps intravascular volume low, so blood pressure is normal or low despite markedly elevated renin and aldosterone. This is a key distinguishing feature from primary hyperaldosteronism.

faqs.question: Does Bartter syndrome cause kidney damage?
faqs.answer: Types I and II commonly cause medullary nephrocalcinosis from hypercalciuria, which can gradually reduce kidney function over decades. Type IV Bartter syndrome causes progressive chronic kidney disease requiring dialysis or transplantation in adulthood in many patients.

faqs.question: What is indomethacin used for in Bartter syndrome?
faqs.answer: Indomethacin reduces prostaglandin E2, which drives excess renin release and polyuria in Bartter syndrome. It substantially decreases urine output, improves electrolyte balance, and normalizes growth in children with types I-III. It is taken with food and monitored for GI and kidney side effects.

faqs.question: Is Bartter syndrome curable?
faqs.answer: There is no cure for the underlying genetic defect. Treatment controls electrolytes and reduces symptoms, allowing near-normal growth and quality of life in most patients with types I-III. Type IV patients may eventually require renal transplantation, which restores normal kidney tubular function.

faqs.question: Can adults develop Bartter syndrome?
faqs.answer: Most cases are diagnosed in infancy or early childhood, but mild forms — particularly type III (ClC-Kb mutation) and type V (CaSR activating mutation) — can present in older children or adults with unexplained hypokalemia, muscle cramps, and mild alkalosis.

faqs.question: What is the long-term outlook for Bartter syndrome?
faqs.answer: With early diagnosis and consistent treatment, children with types I-III achieve near-normal growth and development. Long-term renal function is generally preserved in types I-III if nephrocalcinosis is controlled. Type IV carries the poorest prognosis with frequent progression to kidney failure. Lifelong specialist follow-up is essential.

symptomKeywords: unexplained low potassium child normal blood pressure
polyuria polydipsia child normal blood pressure
metaDescription: Bartter syndrome: rare inherited kidney disorder causing hypokalemia, alkalosis, and salt wasting with normal blood pressure. Genetics and treatment reviewed.
